title: BVA & Equivalence Partitioning
overview: Essential test design techniques used to systematically derive test cases while reducing the total number of required tests.
whyItMatters: Exhaustive testing is impossible. These techniques help SDETs maximize test coverage with the minimum number of test cases by targeting the most error-prone areas (boundaries).
explanation: Equivalence Partitioning (EP) divides input data into valid and invalid partitions, assuming that the program behaves similarly for all inputs in a partition. Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) is an extension of EP that focuses on testing the boundaries between partitions, as errors are most commonly found at the edges of equivalence classes.
keyPoints: Equivalence Partitioning reduces test cases by selecting one representative value per class.
BVA tests values at the exact boundary, just below, and just above it.
These are black-box test design techniques.
They are most effective when applied to variables with logical or physical ranges.
realWorldExample: For a password length requirement of 8-15 characters, BVA test inputs would be 7, 8, 9 and 14, 15, 16 characters.
commonMistakes: Testing only the exact boundary and forgetting the +/- 1 values.
Using BVA on boolean values where it doesn't apply.
bestPractices: Always combine EP and BVA for comprehensive input validation.
Apply BVA to output domains as well as input domains.
Document the derived partitions and boundaries clearly in test plans.

interviewQuestions.q: If a text box accepts values from 1 to 100, what are the BVA test values?
interviewQuestions.a: The typical BVA values would be 0 (min-1), 1 (min), 2 (min+1), 99 (max-1), 100 (max), and 101 (max+1).

interviewQuestions.q: Why do we test boundary values?
interviewQuestions.a: Because developers often make off-by-one errors (using < instead of <=) at boundaries, making them the most defect-dense areas in software.

revisionSummary: EP reduces test cases by testing partitions; BVA catches edge-case bugs by testing boundaries (min-1, min, min+1, max-1, max, max+1).
